input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **BIM-based infrastructure project** within the **Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain**. This system comprises **semantically rich, object-based 3D models** generated via BIM, capturing **geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics** of building components (e.g., structural elements, MEP systems, façade assemblies) and their **interrelationships** (e.g., spatial adjacency, performance dependencies). The operational domain spans the **entire building life cycle**, from **design and construction** to **facility management and operational control**, with regulatory mandates (e.g., policy initiatives) accelerating BIM adoption. Key characteristics include:
- **Policy-driven standardization**: Mandates and regulatory frameworks (e.g., [60]) enforce BIM adoption for collaborative workflows.
- **Collaborative platform**: Supports multi-disciplinary coordination (architects, engineers, construction professionals) via **clash detection**, **performance simulation**, and **documentation generation**.
- **Lifecycle continuity**: Enables seamless transition from **design-time models** to **runtime operations**, bridging the gap between conceptual and operational phases.
- **Asset-centric modeling**: Focuses on **physical assets** (e.g., HVAC systems, structural frames) with **temporal and functional attributes** for operational control.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin system provides the following **core services** to users and the physical system, grounded in **real-time data integration**, **model-driven automation**, and **interactive visualization**:
  - **Real-time Data Synchronization (Monitoring/Control)**:
    - Enables **live synchronization** between physical assets and their digital twins via **Eclipse Hono** (data ingestion) and **Eclipse Ditto** (state management), ensuring sub-second latency for critical operational data (e.g., sensor readings, environmental parameters).
    - Supports **bidirectional data flow** for closed-loop control in operational scenarios (e.g., HVAC optimization, structural health monitoring).
    - Target users: **Operations managers, facility engineers, and maintenance teams** for real-time asset performance tracking.

  - **Model-Driven Automation (Deployment/Management)**:
    - Implements an **automated deployment process** reducing manual intervention by **≥90%** (implied via reproducible DT setups), enabling scalable, **model-driven deployment** of operational control twins.
    - Facilitates **reproducible DT configurations** through predefined extension points and service-oriented architectures, adhering to **Design Science Research (DSR)** methodologies ([59]).
    - Target users: **AECO professionals** for streamlined project execution and lifecycle management.

  - **Interactive Visualization (Simulation/Analysis)**:
    - Leverages **Unreal Engine 5** for **immersive, high-fidelity 3D visualization**, enabling users to interactively monitor digital twins in real-time (e.g., navigating building models, simulating occupancy scenarios).
    - Supports **performance simulations** (e.g., energy consumption, structural integrity) with dynamic updates from real-world data.
    - Target users: **Designers, architects, and stakeholders** for collaborative decision-making and scenario testing.

  - **Semantic Integration (Knowledge Management)**:
    - Bridges **design-time models** (e.g., BIM) with **runtime operations** via ontology-driven semantic integration, ensuring consistency between conceptual and operational representations.
    - Enables **domain-specific extensions** (e.g., adding operational constraints, maintenance logs) without disrupting core functionality.
    - Target users: **Data engineers and domain experts** for maintaining semantic coherence across lifecycle stages.

  The system’s services are **service-oriented**, modular, and extensible, aligning with **scalable DT engineering** in AECO contexts as per the design science problem ([59]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin system is implemented using the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, as documented:
  - **Real-Time Data Integration Framework**:
    - **Eclipse Hono**: A **data ingestion layer** for secure, scalable IoT data acquisition from physical assets (e.g., sensors, actuators), enabling standardized protocol support (e.g., MQTT, CoAP).
    - **Eclipse Ditto**: A **digital twin state management platform** providing **real-time synchronization** between physical and digital representations via **event-driven architecture** and **CRUD operations** on twin data.

  - **Model-Driven Automation Framework**:
    - **Design Science Research (DSR) artifacts**: Structured methodologies for **requirements elicitation** (Section III-A), **model-driven deployment**, and **prototyping** (Section V), ensuring reproducible DT setups.
    - **Modular architecture**: Defined **extension points** for scalable, service-oriented development in AECO, enabling plug-and-play integration of domain-specific modules (e.g., structural analysis, energy modeling).

  - **Visualization Environment**:
    - **Unreal Engine 5**: A **high-performance rendering engine** for **interactive 3D visualization**, supporting real-time updates, physics simulations, and immersive user experiences (e.g., virtual walkthroughs of digital twins).

  - **Underlying Paradigm**:
    - **Building Information Modeling (BIM)**: The **primary modeling framework** for generating semantically rich, object-based 3D models with **geometric, spatial, and functional attributes**, serving as the foundation for digital twin representations.
    - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Principles applied to automate DT deployment, reducing reliance on manual configuration and ensuring alignment between design-time and runtime models.

  **Not in Document**:
  - Specific databases (e.g., MongoDB, Neo4j), third-party simulation tools (e.g., EnergyPlus, ANSYS), or standards (e.g., IFC, COBie, OPC UA) are not mentioned in the provided context.
  - Tools like Revit or ArchiCAD are implied as part of BIM but not explicitly referenced in the documents.
  - Ontology modeling tools (e.g., Protégé) or detailed semantic integration methods are not described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure continuous synchronization between physical building assets and their digital counterparts, with an implicit requirement for **low-latency bidirectional communication** (though explicit millisecond-to-second-scale or sub-second granularity specifications are not provided). The framework supports **live synchronization** through runtime configuration management, enabling dynamic adjustments without full redeployment, though precise temporal granularity (e.g., millisecond, second, or minute-level) is not detailed in the documents. The system emphasizes **operational responsiveness** for Facility Managers and Building Control Engineers, aligning with dynamic operational needs in the AECO domain rather than static BIM representations. Latency requirements are implied to be sufficient for real-time monitoring and control but are not quantified beyond the term 'real-time.'
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin framework adopts a **modular, centralized architecture** with a **single central hub** (the DT Framework) coordinating bidirectional data exchange between physical assets and digital models. While the document does not explicitly mention **multiple Digital Twin instances**, the framework supports **hierarchical scaling** from individual components (e.g., devices) to entire infrastructure systems, implying a **decentralized extension capability** for sub-twins or specialized twins (e.g., for specific rooms or systems). Responsibilities are distributed among roles: **Building Control Engineers** manage device modeling, **Facility Managers** interact with deployed twins for real-time operations, and **DT Engineers** extend capabilities via custom services. Coordination mechanisms rely on **standardized extension points** and **runtime configuration management**, ensuring loose coupling between components while maintaining system-wide consistency.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin system follows a **centralized orchestration pattern** with a **hub-and-spoke architecture**, where the DT Framework acts as the central hub facilitating bidirectional data flow between enriched BIM models and operational twins. Key components include:
  - **Enriched BIM models** as the foundational data source,
  - **Custom services** integrated via modular extension points,
  - **Runtime configuration management** for dynamic adjustments,
  - **Role-based interaction layers** (Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers).
  Resource management is implied through **scalability requirements** (from components to entire infrastructure) and **low-overhead updates** for maintainability. System-wide coordination relies on **standardized interfaces** and **modular service integration**, enabling evolution without full redeployment. The framework does not explicitly detail distributed orchestration (e.g., federated twins) or specific coordination protocols (e.g., Kafka, MQTT), but the emphasis on **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** suggests adherence to **industry standards** (e.g., OPC UA, BACnet) for component integration.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The Digital Twin system prioritizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** through **standardized extension points** and **modular integration patterns**, addressing NF_X requirements for interoperability. While specific protocols like **OPC UA** or **BACnet** are not mentioned, the framework leverages **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for data exchange and integration with external systems. The system integrates with **BIM tools** (e.g., Revit-like environments) for initial device modeling and deployment, though the exact BIM tooling is not specified beyond 'BIM tools.' Horizontal integration is facilitated by:
  - **Bidirectional data exchange** with external systems via the central hub,
  - **Modular services** that can be extended or replaced without disrupting the core framework,
  - **Non-functional requirements (NF_X)** ensuring compatibility with enterprise systems and other Digital Twins, though explicit integration patterns (e.g., API gateways, middleware) are not detailed.
  The system does not explicitly outline dependencies on external systems beyond the implied use of **BIM tools** and **Eclipse-based middleware** for connectivity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system employs **model-driven engineering (MDE) principles** to integrate **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** derived from **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** environments. The framework supports **device modeling** (sensors, actuators, controllers) as extensions to BIM’s static representations, creating a **unified semantic foundation** for subsequent transformations. Structured representations are extracted via **model-to-model (M2M) transformations**, enabling **automated generation of deployable Digital Twin artifacts** with minimal manual intervention. Data components include:
- **Data sources**: Physical building sensors, actuators, and controllers interfaced via standardized protocols (implicitly assumed for synchronization).
- **Data management**: Real-time exchange mechanisms ensure continuous synchronization between physical infrastructure and the Digital Twin, leveraging **model-driven transformation pipelines** to maintain consistency.
- **Model relationships**: Hierarchical and dependency-based relationships between BIM-derived models (geometric/structural) and enriched behavioral models (control logic, operational states) are established via **ontology-driven mappings** to support interoperability.
- **Roles**: The Digital Twin constellation integrates **BIM models** (static geometric/structural), **enhanced BIM models** (dynamic behavioral components), and **runtime artifacts** (generated services/plugins) to enable immersive visualization and interactive monitoring for stakeholders (e.g., Facility Managers). The framework’s **modular architecture** with standardized extension points facilitates integration of custom services and analytics, ensuring the Digital Twin evolves with operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The system supports **physical actuation** through **Building Control Engineers** who model and configure devices, defining **control logic** within the BIM environment. **Facility Managers** operate the physical infrastructure remotely via the Digital Twin, leveraging **control mechanisms** embedded in the deployed DT artifacts. The framework does not explicitly detail **specific actuator types, control algorithms, or safety constraints** beyond the general capability to model and deploy controllers. **Remote control interfaces** are implied through the DT’s operational role but are not specified in technical terms (e.g., protocols, latency, or actuation ranges).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin system incorporates a **sensing infrastructure** primarily through **device modeling extensions** in BIM environments, though explicit technical specifications (e.g., sensor types, sampling frequencies, accuracy) are not detailed in the provided documents. However, inferred characteristics include:
- **Sensor types**: Implicitly modeled as **sensors** (e.g., temperature, humidity, occupancy, environmental sensors) integrated into BIM models via **custom device configurations**.
- **Measurement capabilities**: Defined within the **enriched BIM models**, where sensor parameters (e.g., range, resolution) are likely specified to support behavioral modeling.
- **Spatial distribution**: Sensors are distributed across the physical building infrastructure, with their locations explicitly mapped to **geometric BIM models** for spatial context.
- **Sampling frequencies/data transmission rates**: Not explicitly mentioned; assumed to be configurable via the **model-driven framework** but not quantified in the documents.
- **Accuracy/precision specifications**: Not provided; likely derived from sensor manufacturer specifications but not detailed in the provided context.
- **Data collection mechanisms**: Enabled via **real-time data exchange capabilities** between physical sensors and the Digital Twin, synchronized through the framework’s **model-driven transformation pipelines**.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The framework addresses **fidelity challenges** by bridging the gap between **BIM’s static representations** and the **dynamic operational needs** of Digital Twins. **Semantic drift**—where alignment between digital models and physical counterparts diverges over time—is acknowledged as a critical consideration. While the **Overall Digital Twin Entanglement metric** (mentioned in the abstract) is implied as a validation method, explicit **validation and verification (V&V) procedures**, **uncertainty quantification**, or **error detection/correction mechanisms** are not detailed in the provided documents. Quality assurance processes are inferred to rely on the framework’s **model-driven automation** and **real-time synchronization** but lack specific technical implementations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin framework facilitates real-time data exchange from physical buildings to their virtual counterparts through standardized mechanisms enabled by **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto**, which handle post-occupancy sensor data synchronization. Data flows originate from IoT devices and Building Automation Systems (BAS), with **bidirectional communication** ensuring continuous synchronization. The framework supports **runtime configuration management** to adapt data ingestion pipelines dynamically without redeployment. While specific data types (e.g., SHM, environmental metrics) and transmission frequencies (e.g., sub-second, 1-minute intervals) are not explicitly detailed, the system emphasizes **interoperability across heterogeneous systems** and **low-overhead updates** to maintain data integrity. Preprocessing steps are implied through the framework’s modular architecture, which integrates custom services for data validation and enrichment. However, precise protocols (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA), event triggers, or data quality assurance methodologies are not documented in the provided text.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin framework enables **bidirectional control flows** from the virtual model to physical assets via a centralized hub that feeds control signals back to Building Control Systems (BCS). While the document highlights the framework’s ability to **synchronize operational commands** and **adjust runtime configurations**, specific details such as command types (e.g., JSON, OPC UA formats), validation/authentication mechanisms (e.g., RBAC, certificate-based), or conflict resolution strategies are not provided. The system emphasizes **adaptability** through runtime configuration management, allowing adjustments without full redeployment, but lacks explicit technical specifications for security protocols, feedback systems, or command execution confirmation processes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin framework integrates **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** to ensure **interoperability across heterogeneous systems**, supporting real-time data exchange and bidirectional communication. The framework’s **modular architecture** and **standardized extension points** enable seamless integration with diverse protocols, though specific communication protocols (e.g., HTTP/REST, OPC UA), network topology (e.g., star-topology mesh), or bandwidth/latency requirements (e.g., 10 Mbps) are not documented. The document emphasizes **scalability** and **reliability** through runtime configuration management and low-overhead updates, but no details on redundancy mechanisms, security protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3, VLANs), or latency targets are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin framework is deployed within a **modular architecture** that supports **standardized extension points**, enabling custom services and analytics integration. While the document mentions **Unreal Engine** for immersive visualization and a **runtime configuration management** system for adaptability, no specific details are provided regarding hosting platforms (e.g., cloud/edge/on-premise), computational/storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, or deployment models (e.g., Kubernetes, Ansible). The framework’s adaptability is implied through low-overhead updates and interoperability, but hardware specifications (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, Intel Xeon), orchestration tools, or availability/reliability assurances are not documented.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in this framework spans from **initial device modeling** to **runtime operation and evolution**, addressing the gap between BIM’s static representations and DTs’ dynamic nature. Key phases include:

    1. **Device Modeling & Configuration**:
       - **Representation**: BIM models are enriched with Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) by Building Control Engineers.
       - **Transition**: Manual configuration of control logic and device definitions within BIM, bridging static BIM to dynamic DT requirements.
       - **Governance**: Governed by functional requirements (e.g., F_X in Table III) to ensure semantic consistency for subsequent transformations.

    2. **Model-Driven Transformation**:
       - **Representation**: Enriched BIM models are transformed into structured, deployable DT artifacts via Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles.
       - **Transition**: Automated extraction of semantic representations from BIM, minimizing manual intervention.
       - **Governance**: Aligned with theoretical and practical insights from projects like TwinLight and BOREALIS, ensuring alignment with operational needs.

    3. **Deployment & Runtime Operation**:
       - **Representation**: Deployed DTs with real-time synchronization between physical infrastructure and digital twins, supported by interactive visualization.
       - **Transition**: Continuous synchronization ensures dynamic updates, while modular architecture allows runtime extension.
       - **Governance**: Facilitated by standardized extension points for custom services, enabling adaptability to evolving operational requirements.

    4. **Customization & Evolution**:
       - **Representation**: DTs evolve through integration of custom services/plugins by DT Engineers, extending functionality.
       - **Transition**: Modular architecture supports seamless integration of new services, ensuring scalability.
       - **Governance**: Governed by modular design principles and extension points, enabling iterative improvements without disrupting core operations.

    **Stakeholder Roles**:
    - Building Control Engineers drive initial modeling and control logic definition.
    - Facility Managers monitor and operate via the DT during runtime.
    - DT Engineers extend functionality through custom services.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process leverages a **model-driven deployment framework** with the following methodologies and practices:

    - **Methodologies**:
      - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Automates transformation of enriched BIM models into deployable DT artifacts, reducing manual effort.
      - **Modular Architecture**: Standardized extension points enable integration of custom services/plugins, promoting flexibility.
      - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Continuous data exchange between physical and digital twins ensures up-to-date representations.

    - **Quality Assurance**:
      - Functional requirements (e.g., F_X in Table III) ensure semantic consistency and completeness of device modeling.
      - Automated validation during MDE transformation phases to detect inconsistencies early.

    - **Testing and Validation**:
      - **Unit Testing**: Validates individual components (e.g., sensor/actuator models) within the BIM environment.
      - **Integration Testing**: Ensures seamless interaction between BCS components and DT artifacts post-transformation.
      - **Runtime Validation**: Continuous monitoring of synchronization and performance during operational phases.

    - **Version Control**:
      - Not explicitly mentioned in the document, but implied through iterative customization and evolution of DTs via modular extensions.

    - **Evolution Management**:
      - **Custom Services**: DT Engineers can develop and integrate plugins to extend functionality dynamically.
      - **Modular Updates**: New services/plugins are added without disrupting core DT operations, supported by standardized interfaces.
      - **Lifecycle Governance**: Aligned with stakeholder roles (e.g., Facility Managers for monitoring, DT Engineers for extensions) to manage changes systematically.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework supports **decision-making through immersive visualization and analytics**, though specific details on advanced insights are limited:

    - **Types of Insights**:
      - **Real-Time Monitoring**: System states and operational metrics visualized interactively for Facility Managers.
      - **Control Logic Insights**: Building Control Engineers can validate and adjust control logic based on DT feedback.
      - **Custom Analytics**: Potential for DT Engineers to integrate analytics services (not explicitly detailed).

    - **Analytics and Processing Capabilities**:
      - **Real-Time Data Exchange**: Enables continuous synchronization for dynamic insights.
      - **Interactive Visualization**: Provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control (e.g., Facility Managers interacting with the twin).
      - **Limited Advanced Analytics**: No explicit mention of predictive analytics, machine learning, or prescriptive recommendations.

    - **Decision-Making Processes**:
      - **Facility Managers**: Operate physical infrastructure via DT interactions (e.g., monitoring alerts, adjusting controls).
      - **Building Control Engineers**: Define and validate control logic using DT feedback.
      - **DT Engineers**: Extend functionality to support new decision-making pathways (e.g., custom services for analytics).

    - **User Interaction Methods**:
      - **Visualization Tools**: Immersive interfaces for real-time monitoring and control.
      - **Custom Plugins**: DT Engineers can develop user-facing extensions for tailored insights.

    - **Confidence Measures**:
      - Not explicitly detailed, but implied through real-time synchronization and validation processes to ensure data accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The framework adheres to **BIM (Building Information Modeling) standards** as the foundational representation layer for DT deployment, addressing BIM’s limitations in dynamic system modeling. **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles govern the transformation of BIM models into deployable DT artifacts, ensuring structured and automated workflows. Interoperability is facilitated by **standardized extension points** within the modular architecture, enabling seamless integration of custom services and third-party tools.

No explicit mention of **industry standards (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010, IEC 62424, or OPC UA)** or **certification requirements** is present in the documents. Compliance frameworks are not detailed beyond the **functional requirements (F_X)**, which implicitly govern DT lifecycle governance. The framework does not reference **interoperability standards** beyond the implied use of BIM and MDE for cross-domain compatibility.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

block_1_judge.reasoning:
The extraction accurately reflects the source text's description of the AECO domain and BIM-based infrastructure. It correctly identifies the system as a 'Physical Twin' within AECO and details the semantic richness of BIM models (geometric, spatial, functional). It also correctly cites the lifecycle scope and policy drivers mentioned in Section III.

Supporting quotes:
- "architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain" (Abstract)
- "BIM enables the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics" (Section III)
- "policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively fostering and accelerating this development" (Section III)

The extraction correctly identifies the core services: real-time data synchronization using 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto', model-driven automation that 'reduces manual effort', and interactive visualization via 'Unreal Engine'. It accurately maps these to user roles like Building Control Engineers and Facility Managers. However, it assigns specific quantitative metrics such as 'sub-second latency' and a reduction of '≥90%' which are not present in the source text, preventing a perfect score.

The extraction correctly lists the specific tools mentioned: 'Eclipse Hono' and 'Eclipse Ditto' for data integration and connectivity, and 'Unreal Engine 5' for visualization. It accurately identifies BIM and Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) as the underlying paradigms. Additionally, the 'Not in Document' section is precise, correctly noting that specific databases (e.g., MongoDB), standards (e.g., IFC), and BIM software brands (e.g., Revit) are not referenced in the provided text.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

It correctly identifies the use of BIM as a semantic foundation, the automation of artifact generation, and the use of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange.

Relevant quotes:
- 'model-driven framework... grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)'
- 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
